coredata.dc:title: Remaining useful life prediction of lithium-ion batteries based on POA-SVR method in experimental platforms
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Jianyong
Du, Xiuwen
Yu, Yanxue
Wu, Min
Nian, Heng
Yang, Lei
coredata.dc:description: In experimental platforms with energy storage units, the performance degradation of energy storage batteries has become a key issue restricting the operational effectiveness of the platforms. To improve the accuracy in predicting the remaining useful life (RUL) of batteries in such scenarios, this paper proposes a lightweight, interpretable and small-sample-friendly hybrid prediction method based on the pelican optimization algorithm support vector regression (POA-SVR). Firstly, by analysing the full lifecycle charging and discharging data of energy storage batteries, a multi-dimensional degradation feature set is constructed. Combined with Pearson correlation analysis, feature dimensionality reduction is achieved while retaining key degradation information, effectively solving the curse of dimensionality problem caused by high-dimensional features. By introducing the POA to adaptively optimize the penalty factor and kernel parameters, a prediction model with dynamic parameter adjustment capabilities is established, which significantly improves the degradation trajectory fitting ability. Based on the NASA dataset, experiments are carried out by comparison with other optimization algorithms, which show that the value of the mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) of the POA-SVR remains below 0.7 %, and the value of root mean square error (RMSE) remains below 0.02, achieving higher prediction accuracy and demonstrating the high robustness and reliability even with small samples. It can be concluded that the proposed method offers an interpretable and low-data-dependent lifespan prediction tool for health assessment of energy storage experimental platforms, extending their effective operational cycles.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Energy storage battery
Remaining useful life
POA-SVR algorithm
Multidimensional feature vector

Multidimensional feature vector analysis and extraction
Performance comparison of the optimization algorithm

Comprehensive Performance Comparison of the POA-SVR and SVR.
Comparison of Prediction Errors of Different Models.

head.ce:title: Remaining useful life prediction of lithium-ion batteries based on POA-SVR method in experimental platforms

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Electrical Engineering, Zhejiang University, Hangzhou 310027, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Electrical Engineering, Zhejiang University, Hangzhou 310027, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Grid Zhejiang Electric Power Co. Ltd., Yuhuan Power Supply Company, Taizhou 317100, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Grid Zhejiang Electric Power Co. Ltd.

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Grid Zhejiang Electric Power Co. Ltd., Yuhuan Power Supply Company, Taizhou 317100, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In experimental platforms with energy storage units, the performance degradation of energy storage batteries has become a key issue restricting the operational effectiveness of the platforms. To improve the accuracy in predicting the remaining useful life (RUL) of batteries in such scenarios, this paper proposes a lightweight, interpretable and small-sample-friendly hybrid prediction method based on the pelican optimization algorithm support vector regression (POA-SVR). Firstly, by analysing the full lifecycle charging and discharging data of energy storage batteries, a multi-dimensional degradation feature set is constructed. Combined with Pearson correlation analysis, feature dimensionality reduction is achieved while retaining key degradation information, effectively solving the curse of dimensionality problem caused by high-dimensional features. By introducing the POA to adaptively optimize the penalty factor and kernel parameters, a prediction model with dynamic parameter adjustment capabilities is established, which significantly improves the degradation trajectory fitting ability. Based on the NASA dataset, experiments are carried out by comparison with other optimization algorithms, which show that the value of the mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) of the POA-SVR remains below 0.7 %, and the value of root mean square error (RMSE) remains below 0.02, achieving higher prediction accuracy and demonstrating the high robustness and reliability even with small samples. It can be concluded that the proposed method offers an interpretable and low-data-dependent lifespan prediction tool for health assessment of energy storage experimental platforms, extending their effective operational cycles.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the global energy structure transition, the construction of new-type power systems has become a critical mission for achieving sustainable development, where the energy storage technology is playing an increasingly vital role for balancing energy supply-demand and enhancing the grid flexibility and reliability. Whether it is the large-scale integration of renewable energy or the optimized operation of smart grids, energy storage systems serve as an indispensable component (Yan et al., 2024)- (Li et al., 2022). Therefore, the application scenarios of energy storage devices in teaching and research experiments are rich, the energy storage unit becomes one of the core components in various experimental platforms, like microgrids and integrated energy systems (Zhang et al., 2025). However, with repeated usage of energy storage batteries, their lifespan gradually degrades. Failure to timely replace batteries could significantly compromise system reliability, particularly in experimental settings where the status of energy storage equipment directly impacts both the normal conduct of experimental activities and the validity of research results. However, due to the limited lifespan data obtainable from experimental platforms, balancing prediction accuracy and computational efficiency under small sample conditions has become a core challenge, which affects research and operational decision-making. Therefore, designing efficient prediction methods for the remaining useful life (RUL) of energy storage batteries not only holds significant theoretical value, but also plays a crucial role in practical teaching and scientific researches, which can provide scientific guidance for experimental design and equipment maintenance (Wang et al., 2021)- (Zhu and Hao, 2023).

Regarding current researches on the state of health (SOH) of batteries, the main prediction methods are broadly categorized into two groups: RUL prediction methods based on physics-based failure models (Braun et al., 2022) and data-driven methods (Xue et al., 2020).

Physics-based failure model approaches for battery lifespan prediction rely on information such as battery material properties, load conditions, and failure mechanisms (Xu et al., 2021). These methods estimate remaining battery lifespan by constructing electrochemical models, equivalent circuit models or Brownian motion models (Lyu et al., 2017)- (Lin and Chen, 2018). However, due to the highly nonlinear nature of battery degradation influenced by multiple variables, these models are often integrated with filtering algorithms to simulate dynamic degradation processes and enhance prediction accuracy and stability, such as the Kalman filters (Plett, 2004) and particle filters (Saha et al., 2009). Say concretely, (Ng et al., 2009) proposed an enhanced Coulomb counting method to estimate the RUL of lithium-ion batteries, (Fan et al., 2018) developed a simplified electrochemical model specifically for lifespan prediction in energy storage applications, (Wassiliadis, 2018) employed a dual extended Kalman filter for the RUL prediction of batteries. Despite their theoretical rigor, the physics-based methods mainly rely on the precision of the battery failure model, which complicates prediction efforts and limits practical applicability.

With the rapid advancement of artificial intelligence and big data technology, data-driven methods have demonstrated great potential in the field of battery life prediction. Based on the advanced machine learning and data mining techniques, these methods can explore the deep features of battery performance evolution in a non-invasive manner, thereby revealing the inherent patterns of battery lifespan evolution. Currently, typical data-driven algorithms include the Naive Bayes (Feng and Xiang, 2023)- (Liu et al., 2023), neural networks (Zhang et al., 2022), Gaussian process regression (GPR) (Xu et al., 2020), deep learning (Pang et al., 2023), and support vector regression (SVR) (Liu et al., 2023). Specifically, (Wang et al., 2025) adopted an improved hyperparameter Bayesian optimization combined with a bidirectional long short-term memory network (BiLSTM), and achieved noise correction of the network model through an unscented Kalman filter, greatly improving the accuracy and robustness of the state of charge (SOC) estimation; (Wang et al., 2023) proposed an improved anti-noise adaptive long short-term memory (ANA-LSTM) neural network model, which combines the adaptive state parameter feedback correction strategy to achieve strong robustness in predicting the RUL of lithium-ion batteries; (Wang et al., 2024a) utilized the multiple feature-electrochemical thermal coupling modeling technology to effectively improve the accuracy and adaptability of performance prediction of lithium-ion batteries at low-temperature with real-time correction coefficients and dynamic state space equations. Additionally, (Yu et al., 2025) proposed a rapid search for optimal battery materials and structures under multiple objective constraints, providing an automated strategy for high energy density and long-life design (Wang et al., 2024b). utilized a large model to accelerate the high-throughput screening and prediction performance of novel positive and negative electrodes as well as solid-state electrolytes.

Moreover, the material is a key factor, which influences the battery lifespan. With the advancements in materials, the development of health prediction for batteries would be driven. Advanced data-driven methods applied for the future materials and devices could also be valuable (Ren et al., 2025). In (Lv et al., 2022), a heterogeneous category of AI technology for predicting and discovering battery materials and estimating the state of the battery system is reviewed. Goswami et al. (2023) discussed the development of AI in material processing, explored the structure and material property, and measured the performance of materials in various aspects. Raut et al. (2025) concluded the machine learning in semiconductor device modeling to anticipate device behavior with high accuracy and minimal reliance on physical approximations.

Although the above researches have made significant progress, the SVR algorithm has attracted much attention in practical scenarios with limited experimental data due to its excellent small sample generalization performance and robustness. However, considering that the performance of the SVR algorithm is highly dependent on the selection of hyperparameters, traditional grid search methods are difficult to balance search efficiency and global optimality, which severely limits the further improvement of prediction accuracy. Therefore, researchers attempt to use heuristic algorithms for hyperparameter optimization. Qin et al. (2015) employed particle swarm optimization (PSO) to optimize SVR kernel parameters and developed a battery RUL prediction model, but did not address the redundancy issue of multidimensional feature correlations Yang et al. (2023) applied the grey wolf optimizer (GWO) to enhance SVR for battery RUL prediction, but did not consider the impact of capacity regeneration noise on small sample stability Meng et al. (2022) constructed a healthy lifespan model based on the whale optimization algorithm (WOA), but it is limited to a single heuristic search, which can easily fall into local optimal and lacks systematic constraints on the risk of the dimension explosion in high-dimensional feature spaces.

To address the above shortcomings, this paper proposes a POA-SVR model based on the combination of pelican optimization algorithm (POA) and SVR. The model parameters are efficiently determined through the pelican optimization mechanism, and feature dimensionality reduction is achieved by combining Pearson correlation analysis, thus to solve the direct observation difficulty caused by regenerative capacity interference during battery capacity degradation. The prediction framework is constructed through the following technical pathway. Firstly, health indicators characterizing battery performance degradation and full lifecycle feature vectors are extracted from parameters monitored by the battery management system (BMS). Subsequently, Pearson correlation analysis is employed to reduce the dimensionality of multi-dimensional features, effectively mitigating the risk of dimensionality explosion and improving computational efficiency. Finally, simulation experiments using a small-sample training set comprising 40 % of the data are carried out to validate the performance of the proposed model.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design of the POA-SVR prediction model

ce:section.ce:para: Compared to traditional statistical learning theory, the SVR algorithm exhibits significant advantages in addressing small-sample and nonlinear regression problems, which has been extensively applied in the field of battery RUL prediction. The fundamental principle of the SVR algorithm can be described as follows.

Given a training set:

(3)

(4)

j ) represents the kernel function, and K(x

,x) can transform low-dimensional linear inseparable problems into high-dimensional linear separable problems through inner product operations. The most commonly used kernel function is the radial basis function (RBF), defined as:
(8)

∥
2
)

where g represents the kernel radius. In the SVR algorithm, the values of the penalty coefficient c and kernel radius g critically influence the prediction performance. Specifically, an inappropriate selection of the penalty coefficient may degrade the generalization capability of the model, while the kernel radius governs the sensitivity to local features, thereby affecting both training efficiency and prediction speed. However, there remains a lack of systematic guidelines or established methodologies for determining optimal values of c and g, posing a significant challenge for practical implementations.

Considering that the SVR algorithm suffers from difficulties in hyperparameter selection, utilizing heuristic optimization algorithms to select hyperparameters provides an effective solution. By simulating the natural hunting behavior of pelicans, the POA achieves stronger exploitation capabilities in local search and superior convergence toward the global optimum compared to traditional algorithms.
The implementation steps of the POA algorithm are depicted as follows:
(1) POA initialization

Population members are randomly initialized within the lower and upper bounds of SVR hyperparameters, where each member represents a candidate solution:
(9)
x
i , j
=
l
j
+ r a n d ⋅
(
u
j
−
l
j
)
, i = 1 , 2 , … , N , j = 1 , 2 , … , m

where x
i,j denotes the value of the j variable in the ith candidate solution, N represents the total population size, m is the number of problem variables (here m=2, corresponding to the c and g), rand() denotes the random number uniformly distributed in the interval [0,1], l
j is the lower bound of the jth problem variable, and u
j is the upper bound of the jth problem variable. The POA population matrix is written as (10), where each row of the matrix represents a candidate solution, and each column corresponds to the values of the problem variables.
(10)
X =

where X is the population matrix of pelicans, X
i represents the ith pelican, and each population member represents a candidate solution for SVR hyperparameters. The objective function of the given problem is constructed based on each SVR hyperparameter candidate solution, and the objective function vector is determined by the objective function values as follows:
(11)

where F is the objective function vector, and F
i represents the objective function value of the ith candidate solution.
(2) Exploration stage

Pelicans identify the location of the prey and move toward to the designated area. The prey position is randomly generated within the search space, which enhances the exploration capability of the algorithm in the solution space. The movement of pelicans toward the prey follows the formula below:
(12)
x
i , j

1
represents the new position of the ith pelican in the j dimension during the first phase, I is a random number selected as either 1 or 2 for each iteration and each population member. When I= 2, a larger displacement is generated, guiding the member to newer regions of the search space. p
j denotes the prey position in the j dimension, and F
p is the objective function value at the prey position. The objective function is defined as the mean squared error E MSE between the actual state and predicted state of the battery:
(13)

represents the updated new position, and

P
1
denotes the objective function value corresponding to the new position in the first phase.
(3) Exploitation stage

When pelicans reach the water surface, they spread their wings to push fishes upward and collect preys in their pouches. This strategy enables pelicans to capture more fishes in the targeted area. The mathematical model for this behavior is expressed as follows:
(15)
x
i , j
P
2
=
x
i , j
+ R ⋅
(
1 −
t T
)
( 2 ⋅ rand − 1 ) ⋅
x
i , j

where
x
i , j
P
2
represents the new position of the ith pelican in the j dimension during the second phase. R is a constant set to 0.2. The term R(1 −t/T) represents the neighbourhood radius of population members, enabling local searches near each member to converge to better solutions. In the initial iterations, the value of this coefficient is large, ensuring consideration of regions around each member. As the algorithm progresses, the R(1 −t/T) coefficient decreases, reducing the neighbourhood radius of each member. This allows the algorithm to scan regions around each member with smaller and more precise steps, thereby converging toward solutions closer to the global optimum. Here, t denotes the current iteration number, and T is the maximum iteration count. The effective position update rule is defined as follows:
(16)
X
i
′
=
{
X
i
P
2
,
F
i
P
2
<
F
i
X
i
,
F
i
P
2
>
F
i

where X
i
' is the updated new position, and
F
i
P
1
is the objective function value corresponding to the new position in the second stage.

In summary, the mathematical logic of the POA-SVR is illustrated in the Fig. 1. Firstly, the training set S is defined, and the initial SVR model is constructed according to (2), (3) and (4). Subsequently, the POA population is initialized according to (9), and the mean squared error (MSE) fitness of each candidate hyperparameter is calculated via (13). In the iterative phase, the algorithm sequentially executes the updates from (12), (15) and (16), continuously optimizing the hyperparameters and reevaluating the fitness until convergence is achieved or the maximum iteration limit is reached. Finally, the SVR model is retrained with the obtained optimal hyperparameters to output the final prediction results.

Considering the enhanced exploitation capability in local search and superior convergence toward the global optimum of the POA algorithm, the POA algorithm is employed for the optimal selection of SVR hyperparameters. The overall workflow of the POA-SVR battery predictive lifespan model is depicted in Fig. 2. The main steps are as follows:
(1) Data preprocessing

Normalize the sample data. The preprocessed sample data is divided into training set and test set, where the normalization expression is as follows:
(17)
y ′
=
y −
y
min
y
max
−
y
min

where y and
y ′
represent the sample feature values before and after normalization, respectively; y min and y max denote the minimum and maximum values in the sample data, respectively.
(2) Model parameter settings

Considering the balance between optimization efficiency and accuracy, the population dimension is set to 10, the variable is set to 2, the maximum iteration is set to 100, the lower bound is set to 20, and the upper bound is set to 10.
(3) Selection of the fitness function

The MSE between the actual state values and predicted state values is used to establish the fitness function MSE:
(18)
M S E =
1 n
∑ i n
(
y ˆ
i
−
y
i
)
2

where y
i is the actual capacity of the lithium-ion during the ith cycle, and
y ˆ
i
is the predicted capacity of lithium-ion during the ith cycle.
(4) SVR parameters optimization based on the POA algorithm

The POA algorithm is iteratively executed to determine whether the POA-SVR algorithm has reached the maximum iteration count T. If it is satisfied, the POA algorithm outputs the optimal hyperparameter solutions for the penalty coefficient c and kernel radius g. These optimized hyperparameters are then returned to the SVR model to construct the POA-SVR model.
(5) Lithium-ion battery RUL prediction
The test dataset is used to validate the established model. The predicted battery lifespan is compared with the actual lifespan, and the model error accuracy is calculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multidimensional feature vector analysis and extraction

ce:section.ce:para: While previous researches have focused on the development of battery prediction models, the selection of feature vectors is equally critical for accurate RUL prediction. A persistent challenge lies in extracting high-quality indicators from basic parameters such as current, voltage, and temperature to comprehensively reflect the actual health status of batteries.

This section firstly introduces and analyzes the publicly available battery dataset to lay the foundation for subsequent multidimensional feature extraction and RUL model prediction. The data used in this study are from the lithium-ion battery cycling experiment dataset published by NASA Ames Research Center. The experiment involves four lithium-ion batteries with a nominal capacity of 2 A·h, all the batteries are tested under identical ambient temperature of 24°C. Charging and discharging processes are recorded. During charging, the batteries are charged in constant current mode at 1.5 A until the voltage reached the cutoff voltage of 4.2 V. The charging mode then switches to constant voltage until the current drops below 20 mA, marking the end of the charging stage. For discharging, the batteries are discharged at a constant current of 2 A until the voltage reaches the cutoff threshold. Detailed experimental parameters are summarized in Table 1.

Based on the experimental data, the battery capacity degradation curves are shown in Fig. 3. From the curves, it can be observed that the four batteries exhibit varying degrees of capacity degradation. However, lithium-ion batteries demonstrate capacity regeneration capability, leading to a nonlinear relationship between battery capacity and cycle count. It is generally accepted that a battery reaches its end of life (EOL) when its capacity decays to 70 % of the initial value. In this study, for the NASA dataset, the EOL threshold is defined as the capacity dropping from 2 A·h to 1.4 A·h. For battery B0007, since its capacity does not decline to 1.4 A·h, a slightly higher threshold of 1.44 A·h is selected as its EOL value.

During battery operation, parameters such as current, voltage, and temperature can be readily acquired through the BMS, from which seven feature vectors are extracted for RUL prediction. The seven feature vectors are the maximum charging temperature H a, the maximum discharging temperature H b, the average charging temperature H c, the average discharging temperature H d, the constant current charging interval H e, the constant current discharging time H f, and the constant voltage drop time interval H g. The variation curves of the seven feature vectors with cycle count are illustrated in Fig. 4.

When the number of input feature vectors increases, low-correlation features may emerge. These irrelevant features can exponentially increase computational complexity, significantly slow down algorithm execution, exacerbate data sparsity, and potentially lead to dimensionality explosion. Pearson correlation analysis serves as an effective method to evaluate the correlation between each feature vector and capacity. When the original data dimensionality is not excessively high, dimension reduction can be achieved by selecting the most significant features, thereby improving the model computational efficiency.

Given two variables
α =
(
α
1
,
α
2
, ⋯ ,
α
n
)
and
β =
(
β
1
,
β
2
, ⋯ ,
β
n
) , the Pearson correlation coefficient can be expressed as:
(19)
Pearson =
E
(
α β
)
− E
( α )
E
( β )
E
( α )
2
−
( E
( α )
)
2
E
( β )
2
−
( E
( β )
)
2

where E represents the expected value, the Pearson correlation coefficient approaches −1 or 1 when two vectors exhibit a strong linear relationship, while it equals 0 under statistically independent conditions. Table 2 quantitatively demonstrates this relationship by listing Pearson correlation coefficients between the seven feature vectors and battery capacity, which are derived from experimental datasets.

According to the Table 2, the Pearson correlation coefficients between the maximum charging temperature H a, average charging temperature H c, and the battery capacity are only 0.32 and 0.23, respectively, indicating weak correlations. Therefore, H a and H c are unsuitable as model inputs. Although the average discharging temperature H d and constant-current discharging time H f show relatively low correlations with the capacity of battery B0018, they exhibit stronger correlations with capacities of other three battery groups, justifying their retention. In conclusion, H b, H d, H e, H f, and H g are selected to characterize the degradation behaviour of energy storage batteries.

To comparatively analyze the prediction performance of the POA-SVR algorithm, the mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) and root mean square error (RMSE) are selected to assess the prediction accuracy. The MAPE increases linearly with error magnitude, where smaller values indicate superior model performance. Conversely, the RMSE quantifies absolute errors, with RMSE = 0 representing a perfect model fit. Correspondingly, the error values of the MAPE and RMSE can be calculated from (20) and (21).
(20)
E
M A P E
=
1 n
∑ i n
|
y ˆ
i
−
y
i
y
i
|
× 100 %
(21)
E
R M S E
=
1 n
∑ i n
(
y ˆ
i
−
y
i
)
2

The RUL of the battery is defined as the number of cycles from the starting point of prediction to the end-of-life of the battery. The battery end-of-life is reached when its actual capacity attenuates to this threshold, where capacity serves as the most common metric for determining the threshold.

To analyze the error between the predicted lifespan and the actual lifespan, the absolute error E
a is defined as the difference between the actual RUL and the predicted RUL, expressed as:
(22)
E
a
=
R
t u r e
−
R
p r e d
where R
pred denotes the predicted RUL, R
ture represents the actual RUL, and the relative error E
r is defined as:
(23)
E
r
=
|
R
t u r e
−
R
p r e d
|
R
t u r e
× 100 %
where E
r is within the interval [0,1].

Based on the established POA-SVR model and five multidimensional feature vectors, the POA-SVR algorithm is compared with the conventional SVR method here, where c= 1 and g= 0.01.
Fig. 5 to Fig. 8 show the prediction results for four battery groups trained on 50 % of the data, where the red line represents the actual capacity, the blue line denotes the predicted capacity of the POA-SVR, and the yellow line denotes the predicted capacity of the conventional SVR. As can be seen from the four sets of curves, the predicted capacity of the POA-SVR almost perfectly coincide with the actual capacity. In both the early and middle stages of the battery's life, the blue lines accurately capture the slow capacity decay slope of corresponding red lines. It remains synchronized at the end-of-life inflection point, with only slight deviations observed for batteries B0005 and B0007. In contrast, the conventional SVR shows significant lag or prediction advancement in the final stage, failing to accurately track the rapid decay region. This phenomenon indicates that the POA-SVR overcomes the sensitivity of conventional SVR to hyperparameters and local optima through global optimization, and can achieve a high degree of fit when using the complete training dataset. Table 3 shows the hyperparameter values of the POA-SVR and SVR.

Table 4 details the RUL prediction results of the POA-SVR model, denoted as PRUL. In Table 4, the ST denotes the starting point of prediction and T denotes the total cycle count. The results demonstrate that the POA-SVR model successfully predicts RUL values, achieving zero prediction error for battery B0006.

Table 5 summarizes the prediction errors, showing that the POA-SVR model achieves significantly lower MAPE and RMSE values compared to the SVR model. For example, for battery B0018, the SVR method yields a MAPE of 7.14 % and an RMSE of 0.1030, while the POA-SVR model reduces these values to 0.04 % (MAPE) and 0.0043 (RMSE), representing a 7.1 % improvement in MAPE and a 0.099 reduction in RMSE. Overall, the POA-SVR algorithm maintains RMSE values below 0.02 and MAPE values under 0.7 % across all test cases, demonstrating substantial performance enhancement over the unoptimized SVR method.

To further investigate the robustness of the POA-SVR algorithm in predicting with limited sample sizes, select 40 % of the sample data for model training. Fig. 9 to
Fig. 12 show the prediction results for four battery groups trained on 40 % of the data. When the training sample is reduced to 40 %, the POA-SVR prediction curve still maintains a high degree of consistency with the actual curve. The most significant deviation appears only in B0005 and B0018, where the predicted life is slightly longer, with an error of approximately 2 %. The relative error for all batteries is less than or equal to 21 %, and the trend shows no distortion, indicating that the model is not sensitive to fluctuations in sample size. By comparison, the conventional SVR exhibits larger deviations in the same scenario, which further demonstrates the small-sample advantages of the POA-SVR. Table 6 shows the hyperparameter values of the POA-SVR and SVR.

Table 7 shows the RUL prediction results of the POA-SVR model trained on 40 % of the data, and Table 8 summarizes the comparison of prediction errors between the POA-SVR and SVR. As shown in Table 7, under the 40 % training set condition, the relative error E
r of the RUL predicted by the POA-SVR algorithm for all tested battery groups remained strictly below 21 %. Significantly, compared with predictions using 50 % training data, the experimental comparison confirms that the E
r maintained stability without upward deviation, ultimately proving the strong robustness of the POA-SVR even under low-quantity training samples. This consistency highlights its reliability across varying data availability scenarios.

Furthermore, Table 9 summarizes the prediction errors for the POA-SVR and SVR under two training set ratios. As can be seen, the POA-SVR consistently maintains an MAPE below 3 % and an RMSE below 0.041 across all four batteries. Specifically, under the 50 % training set condition, its maximum RMSE is only 0.0183. Even when the training sample is reduced to 40 %, the error is largely controlled within 0.0313, with a performance degradation (increase in RMSE) of no more than 0.02. In contrast, the MAPE for the unoptimized SVR is consistently above 5 %, with its RMSE reaching as high as 0.1950. Furthermore, it is extremely sensitive to the sample ratio. When the training size decreases from 50 % to 40 %, the RMSE for battery B0005 increases by 135 %. This provides strong validation that the global search of the POA algorithm can significantly reduce the hyperparameter sensitivity of the SVR, thereby improving its robustness and generalization capability in small-sample scenarios.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance comparison of the optimization algorithm
ce:section.ce:para: Experiments are carried out to further recognize the superior performance of POA. By choosing the four battery groups trained on 50 % of the data, the corresponding prediction results of the proposed POA-SVR, the PSO-SVR, the GWO-SVR and the unoptimized SVR are depicted in Fig. 13. Meanwhile, the prediction errors for the four models are summarized in Table 10.

From Fig. 13, it can be clearly observed that the prediction curve of the POA-SVR algorithm shows a higher degree of alignment with the trend of the actual RUL data when it approaches the threshold, indicating the improved prediction accuracy in comparison with other algorithms. In contrast, although the PSO-SVR algorithm and the GWO-SVR algorithm can follow the actual trend in certain segments, they exhibit some degree of deviation when approaching critical turning points of the actual RUL. In particularly, the prediction curves of the other algorithms show significant divergence after the starting point of the prediction. This also demonstrates that the POA-SVR algorithm has the better adaptability and the higher prediction accuracy in comparison with other algorithms, which benefits from the effective integration of its feature mapping and optimization strategies. In contrast, other two optimized SVR algorithms may struggle to effectively adapt to the characteristics of the dataset when handling complex non-linear degradation patterns due to the inherent limitations in their model structure and parameter settings, thereby failing to achieve satisfactory accuracy.

Furthermore, the error comparison in Table 10 shows that the MAPE and RMSE values of the POA-SVR algorithm are lower than those of the PSO-SVR, GWO-SVR, and unoptimized SVR algorithms. For the four battery datasets, the POA-SVR consistently achieves lower the MAPE and RMSE values than PSO-SVR. Particularly, for battery B0006, the MAPE of POA-SVR (0.12 %) is significantly lower than that of PSO-SVR (2.27 %), which verifies that the POA algorithm may outperform the PSO algorithm in balancing exploration and exploitation. Besides, the PSO algorithm occasionally converges prematurely to local optimal, whereas the simulated predatory behavior of the POA algorithm likely provides a stronger ability to escape local optima, thereby identifying better SVR parameters and achieving higher prediction accuracy. Compared with the GWO-SVR, the results demonstrate that POA-SVR performs consistently and excellently across multiple datasets, while the GWO-SVR exhibits greater volatility. Overall, the POA-SVR algorithm delivers more reliable comprehensive performance.

Focused on the need for small-sample RUL prediction for energy storage batteries, this paper proposes the POA-SVR algorithm and validates its lightweight RUL prediction framework. The conclusions and innovations are summarized as follows:

1) As for the algorithm, by employing the POA for a global search of the penalty coefficient and kernel function radius, the model overcomes the limitations of empirical parameter tuning in traditional SVR. The iterative process of exploration and exploitation ensures stable convergence while achieving rapid optimization, significantly reducing manual tuning efforts and computational costs while maintaining high accuracy.

2) As for the feature, five highly correlated features are selected from seven initial degradation indicators, reducing the feature dimensionality by 28 %. This method both alleviates the curse of dimensionality and preserves the interpretability of the degradation mechanism.

3) As for the performance, on the NASA dataset, the model achieves prediction accuracies with MAPE no greater than 0.7 % and RMSE no greater than 0.02 under both 50 % and 40 % training sample conditions. The relative error for all four battery sets is under 21 %, significantly outperforming unoptimized SVR and thus validating the robustness and applicability across different operating conditions of the model. Additionally, compared with the PSO-SVR and GWO-SVR model, the prediction curve of the POA-SVR model shows the closer alignment with the actual degradation trajectory and the smaller error fluctuations.

In summary, the POA-SVR framework combines high accuracy, strong interpretability, and low computational complexity. It provides an easy-to-deploy, low-data-dependent life prediction tool for the health management of microgrids, integrated energy systems, and wearable power sources. Future work can focus on incorporating environmental stress and multi-rate discharge factors to enhance the generalization ability of the model under more complex operating conditions.

This work was partially supported by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (Grant No. 226202400244), and the Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. LY23E070002), the National Science Foundation for Distinguished Young Scholars of China (Grant No. 52325702).

Lei Yang: Formal analysis, Data curation. Heng Nian: Supervision, Conceptualization. Min Wu: Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Yanxue Yu: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization. Du Xiuwen: Methodology. Jianyong Zhao: Writing – original draft, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no conflict of interest.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.egyr.2025.11.029.
